---
title: "Submit"
pagetitle: "Submit — Working Papers in Critical Search"
description: "How to submit a paper, dataset, method note, or short piece."
format:
  html:
    page-layout: custom
---

```{=html}
<section class="wpcs-wrap wpcs-masthead">
  <div class="wpcs-masthead__grid">
    <div class="wpcs-micro">Submit<br>Est. 2026</div>
    <div>
      <h1 class="wpcs-page-h1">Submit your work</h1>
      <p class="wpcs-lead">
        Submissions live in their own GitHub repository under our organization,
        with editorial review handled through pull requests. If you'd rather
        send us a draft and let us handle the technical setup, email works too.
      </p>
    </div>
    <div class="wpcs-masthead__vlabel">Paper count: 1</div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="wpcs-wrap wpcs-section">
  <div class="wpcs-section-head">
    <div class="wpcs-micro">№ 01</div>
    <h2>What we're looking for</h2>
    <div class="wpcs-section-head__trailing">Scope and formats</div>
  </div>
  <div class="wpcs-row">
    <div class="wpcs-small-mono" style="line-height: 1.7;">
      Method and interpretation are inseparable. Show your evidentiary chain.
    </div>
    <div>
      <p>
        <em>Working Papers in Critical Search</em> publishes work at the
        intersection of history, computation, and critical approaches to
        political economy, empire, and environment. We are looking for
        scholarship that treats method and interpretation as inseparable:
        submissions should make their evidentiary chains explicit &mdash;
        where sources originate, how they have been transformed, how
        methods have been tested, what the data's limitations are, and
        which passages substantiate the interpretation.
      </p>

      <h3 class="wpcs-subhead" style="margin-top: 28px;">We publish in four formats</h3>
      <ul class="wpcs-prose-list">
        <li><strong>Draft papers</strong> &mdash; something you're developing for a traditional journal. Get feedback, establish priority, iterate in public.</li>
        <li><strong>Dataset papers</strong> &mdash; you built a historical dataset. Document it, share it, get credit for it.</li>
        <li><strong>Method notes</strong> &mdash; you figured out how to do something useful. Write it up so others can learn.</li>
        <li><strong>Short pieces</strong> &mdash; an observation, a benchmark, a comparison. Too small for a journal but worth sharing.</li>
      </ul>

      <h3 class="wpcs-subhead" style="margin-top: 28px;">What falls outside our scope</h3>
      <p>
        We are not the right venue for finished historical essays that
        gesture at computational tools without showing how they were used,
        or for technical demonstrations on historical material without a
        historical argument. We are also not a venue for very early drafts
        seeking developmental feedback before they have an argument;
        working papers should be coherent and citable in their current
        form, even if the underlying research is ongoing.
      </p>

      <p class="wpcs-cite-note" style="margin-top: 16px;">
        For the broader intellectual rationale, see the
        <a href="https://working-papers-in-critical-search.github.io/paper-001-introduction/">introduction to the series</a>
        and the
        <a href="https://working-papers-in-critical-search.github.io/home/about.html">About</a>
        page.
      </p>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="wpcs-band">
  <div class="wpcs-wrap">
    <div class="wpcs-section-head">
      <div class="wpcs-micro">№ 02</div>
      <h2>How to submit</h2>
      <div class="wpcs-section-head__trailing">Five steps</div>
    </div>
    <div class="wpcs-row">
      <div class="wpcs-small-mono" style="line-height: 1.7;">
        Each paper is its own repository inside our organization. You write
        on a branch, open a pull request when ready, and editors review in
        the PR.
      </div>
      <div>
        <ol class="wpcs-steps">
          <li>
            <div class="wpcs-micro">Step 01 &mdash; Get access</div>
            <p>
              Email an editor with a one-paragraph description of the work
              and your GitHub username. We'll either add you to the
              <code>Working-Papers-in-Critical-Search</code> organization or, for
              one-off submissions, add you as a collaborator on a single repo.
              This is a one-time step; repeat contributors don't need to ask again.
            </p>
          </li>
          <li>
            <div class="wpcs-micro">Step 02 &mdash; Create your paper repo</div>
            <p>
              From the
              <a href="https://github.com/Working-Papers-in-Critical-Search/paper-template">paper template</a>,
              click <strong>Use this template &rarr; Create a new repository</strong>.
              In the <em>Owner</em> dropdown choose
              <code>Working-Papers-in-Critical-Search</code> &mdash; not your personal
              account. Name it <code>paper-your-short-title</code>.
            </p>
            <p style="margin-top: 12px;">
              Then on the new repo: <strong>Settings &rarr; Pages &rarr; Source: GitHub Actions</strong>.
              The first push will trigger a build; without this setting the
              site won't deploy.
            </p>
          </li>
          <li>
            <div class="wpcs-micro">Step 03 &mdash; Write on a branch</div>
            <p>
              Clone the repo and create a draft branch &mdash; <code>draft</code>,
              <code>v1</code>, or whatever you like. Edit <code>index.qmd</code>;
              structure it however makes sense. Preview locally with
              <code>quarto preview</code>. Push as you go.
            </p>
            <p style="margin-top: 12px;">
              Working on a branch keeps the published <code>main</code> clean while
              you iterate. The Pages site only reflects what's been merged.
            </p>
          </li>
          <li>
            <div class="wpcs-micro">Step 04 &mdash; Open a pull request</div>
            <p>
              When you're ready for editorial review, open a PR from your
              draft branch into <code>main</code>. Editors review in the PR
              itself: line comments on specific passages, suggested edits, and
              requested changes. Iterate on the same branch &mdash; new commits
              update the PR automatically. This is gatekeeping for fit and
              clarity, not three anonymous reviewers.
            </p>
          </li>
          <li>
            <div class="wpcs-micro">Step 05 &mdash; Merge, release, DOI</div>
            <p>
              Once the PR is approved and merged, the Pages site rebuilds and
              the paper is live. We tag a release (<code>v1.0</code>); Zenodo
              archives the snapshot and mints a DOI. Editors then add the
              paper to the <a href="papers.html">papers index</a>.
            </p>
            <p style="margin-top: 12px;" class="wpcs-cite-note">
              First-ever Pages deploy on a fresh repo can take 5&ndash;7 minutes
              before the URL resolves. Subsequent deploys are quick.
            </p>
          </li>
        </ol>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="wpcs-wrap wpcs-section">
  <div class="wpcs-section-head">
    <div class="wpcs-micro">№ 03</div>
    <h2>What editorial review looks like</h2>
    <div class="wpcs-section-head__trailing">In the PR</div>
  </div>
  <div class="wpcs-row">
    <div class="wpcs-small-mono" style="line-height: 1.7;">
      Conversations happen on specific lines, not in email threads.
    </div>
    <div>
      <p>
        Once your PR is open, an editor reads through and leaves comments
        directly on the lines that need attention. Some comments are
        questions; others are concrete suggestions you can accept with a
        single click. You respond with new commits to the same branch &mdash;
        no new PR, no resubmission. Conversation continues in the same place
        until everyone's happy.
      </p>
      <p style="margin-top: 16px;">
        We're not running blind peer review. Editorial review here checks
        scope fit, asks whether the methods are documented well enough that a
        reader could follow them, and flags places where the argument or the
        evidence chain has a gap. For papers that touch areas outside the
        editors' direct expertise, we may invite a third reader into the PR.
      </p>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="wpcs-wrap wpcs-section">
  <div class="wpcs-section-head">
    <div class="wpcs-micro">№ 04</div>
    <h2>What we need from you</h2>
    <div class="wpcs-section-head__trailing">Code &middot; data &middot; compute</div>
  </div>
  <div class="wpcs-row">
    <div class="wpcs-small-mono" style="line-height: 1.7;">
      Transparency, not one-click reproducibility.
    </div>
    <div>
      <h3 class="wpcs-subhead">Code</h3>
      <p>
        Include your code or describe your methods. It does not need to run on a
        reviewer's laptop. If you used a cluster with H100s for thirty hours, just
        say so.
      </p>

      <h3 class="wpcs-subhead">Data</h3>
      <ul class="wpcs-prose-list">
        <li><strong>Small data</strong> (&lt; 10 MB): include it in a <code>data/</code> folder</li>
        <li><strong>Large data</strong>: host on Zenodo, Hugging Face, or another DOI repository and link to it</li>
        <li><strong>Sensitive data</strong>: describe what you used and how others can access comparable sources</li>
      </ul>

      <h3 class="wpcs-subhead">Compute</h3>
      <p>
        We welcome work at any scale &mdash; laptop, single GPU, or a cluster with weeks
        of runtime. Just be clear about what you used so readers can calibrate.
      </p>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="wpcs-wrap wpcs-section">
  <div class="wpcs-section-head">
    <div class="wpcs-micro">№ 05</div>
    <h2>After publication</h2>
    <div class="wpcs-section-head__trailing">Living documents</div>
  </div>
  <div class="wpcs-row">
    <div class="wpcs-small-mono" style="line-height: 1.7;">
      Papers are not frozen. Open another PR any time.
    </div>
    <div>
      <p>
        Minor fixes &mdash; typos, broken links, small clarifications &mdash;
        come in as small PRs that merge quickly and trigger a rebuild. Major
        revisions warrant a new version (<code>v1.1</code>, <code>v2.0</code>),
        a new release tag, and a new Zenodo DOI. Previous versions remain
        citeable at their original DOIs.
      </p>
      <p class="wpcs-cite-note" style="margin-top: 20px;">
        Optional: if a paper has strong technical contributions, we can help submit
        a PDF snapshot to arXiv.
      </p>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="wpcs-wrap wpcs-section">
  <div class="wpcs-section-head">
    <div class="wpcs-micro">№ 06</div>
    <h2>Other paths in</h2>
    <div class="wpcs-section-head__trailing">Email &middot; transfer</div>
  </div>
  <div class="wpcs-row">
    <div class="wpcs-small-mono" style="line-height: 1.7;">
      Two alternatives if the main path doesn't fit.
    </div>
    <div>
      <h3 class="wpcs-subhead">Email an editor (no Git required)</h3>
      <p>Send your materials and we'll set up the repository for you:</p>
      <ul class="wpcs-prose-list">
        <li>Your notebook (<code>.qmd</code>, <code>.ipynb</code>, <code>.Rmd</code>, or Word + code files)</li>
        <li>Any small data files</li>
        <li>Author info (name, affiliation, ORCID if you have one)</li>
      </ul>
      <p style="margin-top: 12px;">
        <a href="mailto:jim.clifford@usask.ca">jim.clifford@usask.ca</a> &middot;
        <a href="mailto:jo.guldi@emory.edu">jo.guldi@emory.edu</a>
      </p>

      <h3 class="wpcs-subhead" style="margin-top: 32px;">Already drafting in your own GitHub account?</h3>
      <p>
        If you've already created the repo under your personal account from
        the template, you can transfer it to the organization when ready
        (<strong>Settings &rarr; Danger Zone &rarr; Transfer ownership</strong>,
        destination <code>Working-Papers-in-Critical-Search</code>). Two caveats
        worth knowing:
      </p>
      <ul class="wpcs-prose-list">
        <li><strong>Wait at least 15 minutes after your first Pages deploy before initiating the transfer.</strong> GitHub holds an internal lock on freshly-deployed repos and the web UI fails silently with "Something went wrong!" during that window.</li>
        <li>If a transfer attempt seems to hang in "Moving repository to..." for more than a few minutes, treat it as failed and email an editor &mdash; we can finish the move from the org side.</li>
      </ul>
      <p style="margin-top: 12px;">
        Whenever possible, prefer the create-directly-in-org path in §02.
        It's strictly simpler.
      </p>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="wpcs-wrap wpcs-section wpcs-section--flush">
  <div class="wpcs-submit-cta">
    <div class="wpcs-micro">Questions</div>
    <h3>Open an issue on GitHub or email the editors directly.</h3>
    <a href="https://github.com/Working-Papers-in-Critical-Search/home/issues" class="wpcs-mono wpcs-submit-cta__btn">Open an issue &rarr;</a>
  </div>
</section>
```
